summary: This notebook demonstrates a memory-efficient pipeline for social action recognition in mice, addressing five core competition challenges including variable mouse counts, missing labels, and out-of-memory tracking data. It converts raw Cartesian coordinates into rotation- and translation-invariant distance features, handles sparse multi-label targets via independent binary classifiers, and processes data in streaming batches. Readers learn how to adapt tree-based models to complex video tracking data while strictly adhering to submission constraints and memory limits.
competition_flow: Reads training and test CSVs alongside parquet tracking/annotation files, generates batched distance-based features for single-mouse and mouse-pair interactions, trains per-action binary LightGBM classifiers using GroupKFold cross-validation, and outputs a rule-compliant submission CSV.
data_reading: Loads metadata via pd.read_csv for train.csv and test.csv
Reads tracking and annotation data via pd.read_parquet from Kaggle input directories, gracefully skipping files with missing annotations
data_processing: Subsamples training data to 100k samples via a custom TrainOnSubsetClassifier wrapper
Processes test data in memory-efficient generators to avoid OOM errors
Normalizes coordinates to centimeters using pix_per_cm_approx
Drops noisy headpiece and tail body parts when more than five are tracked
Imputes missing values with SimpleImputer and applies a post-processing step to enforce non-overlapping predictions and full video coverage
features_engineering: Computes Euclidean distances between all pairs of tracked body parts for translation and rotation invariance
Adds speed features by comparing coordinates shifted by 10 frames
Separates features into single-mouse and mouse-pair interaction sets
cv_strategy: GroupKFold by video_id for out-of-fold binary classifier evaluation
insights: Distance-based features provide necessary translation and rotation invariance for recognizing animal behaviors across varying positions.
Missing multi-class labels can be effectively handled by training independent binary classifiers per action and applying a probability threshold.
Highly redundant 30 FPS tracking data can be safely subsampled without losing predictive signal, enabling out-of-memory processing.
Submission robustness requires strict deduplication of overlapping frame predictions and explicit coverage of all test videos.
critical_findings: Videos tracked with more than five body parts contain noisy headpiece and tail components that degrade performance and should be filtered.
Certain lab datasets (e.g., MABe22) lack annotation files entirely and must be excluded during training to prevent crashes.
The competition scoring metric requires careful handling of sparse labels, as predictions for unlabelled agent-target pairs must be ignored.
